title: SCREEN Act
plain_summary: The SCREEN Act creates new rules for US movie companies that want help from the State Department to make films. These companies must tell the government if they have sent their movies to Chinese officials for approval. They also have to promise they will not change their movies just because China asks them to. The State Department must write a report every year to Congress about how movie companies are dealing with China.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for considera

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Stopping Communist Regimes from Engaging in Edits Now Act or the SCREEN Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Limitation on Use of Funds for Production of Films and Prohibition on Use of Such Funds for Films Subject to Conditions on Content or Altered for Screening in the People's Republic of China or at the Request of the Chinese Communist Party
sections.text: The Secretary of State can only give technical support or let companies use State Department property to help make movies if the US company agrees to certain conditions. The company must give the Secretary a list of all movies they made in the past ten years where they sent the movie content to Chinese government officials or Chinese Communist Party officials to get approval to show the movie in China. For each movie on this list, the company must provide the movie title and the date they sent it to China. The company must also sign a written promise that they will not change their movie content because China asked them to or because they think China might ask them to. Finally, the company must give this signed promise to the Secretary. Even if a company follows these rules, the President cannot give any federal government support for making a movie if the movie is made together with a Chinese company that has to follow content rules set by Chinese officials, or if the US company is on a government report showing they changed movie content for China. Every one hundred eighty days after this law starts, and then once every year after that, the Secretary of State must send a report to Congress. This report must describe all movies that US companies told the government about, where the companies got federal support for movie making. The report must list each movie that was sent to Chinese officials for approval, including which US company sent it, the movie title, and when it was sent. The report must also list movies where US companies promised not to change content for China, including the company name and movie title. The report must identify any movies from the past three years where the Secretary thinks the content was actually changed because of Chinese requests or to avoid Chinese complaints. The report must also identify movies from the past ten years where companies both sent content to China and promised not to change it, but the Secretary thinks they changed it anyway because of Chinese influence. In this law, appropriate committees of Congress means the Senate Committee on Foreign Relations and the House Committee on Foreign Affairs. Content means any description of a movie including the script. United States company means a private business that is legally created under US laws or laws of any US state or territory.
